text: The patient was a 30-year-old woman and complained of neck pain and shoulder pain.
I thought that the da Vinci would really give us the visualization and manual dexterity to get it out safely.
I explained to the patient preoperatively
that there was a very likely chance that she would end up with a Horner's syndrome.
I've taken a bunch of these out before and I know that it's very close to the stellate ganglia and a lot of these patients end up with a Horner's and sometimes, most of the time, that's temporary.
Sometimes it can be permanent.
So she understood that going in.
You can see I used three robotic arms.
The ports are placed in about the fifth or sixth inner space.
I'm using the force bipolar in my left hand and I'm using the long curve bipolar in my right hand with the energy.
I'm dividing the parietal pleura here over the mass.
I have an eight millimeter assistant port and that's where the tumor is going to be extracted from.
So basically the tumor is encapsulated and I'm just trying to
come around the capsule.
I don't want to violate the capsule.
The underside of the tumor is pretty easy to dissect and obviously the more technically challenging part is going to be on the cephalad aspect of this tumor.
But I'm just using my sponge roll to do some retraction.
And here we're getting up to the attachment which I believe is the sympathetic chain.
And I'm just trying to encircle it the best I can at this point.
Looks like a little nerve fiber.
We did have a nerve stimulator in the room.
and you'll see that we used it a little bit just to identify which were the nerves, which weren't.
It's important to note that although most of these tumors are benign, if you don't do a complete resection
They can recur.
people describe putting a big stitch right through the middle of these tumors to put caudate retraction on it.
I didn't think that was necessary.
This is just a zero degree scope.
I don't think an angled scope is necessarily beneficial in this in this location.
So there you see, it looks like it's the sympathetic chain.
The mass is coming basically right from the sympathetic chain.
This is where we did our nerve stimulation to see if that was a nerve.
And I'll tell you, I didn't find this piece of equipment all that helpful.
The tech kept saying that she doesn't think that's a nerve,
The way it looked, it certainly appeared that it was.
Still, I understood that in order to do a complete extirpation of the tumor, you're probably going to have to divide that structure.
I'm aware that the subclavian vessels, particularly the subclavian vein, is going to be right near the structure on the cephalad aspect of it.
So I'm just careful.
You can see the subclavian artery in front of it.
Again, we're putting the nerve stimulator right on the structure that appears to be the sympathetic chain.
And the feedback we were getting is it doesn't appear to be a nerve.
At this point, it seems like we got the mass pretty well encircled.
If I'm going to take it out, I'm going to have to start dividing some of these structures.
You can see how this mass is sort of growing from this nerve and there's really no way I can excise the mass without dividing it.
Couple more attachments.
Starting to see the subclavian vein just above at the top of that cavity.
And that's the first rib.
At this point the mass is almost completely encircled and I just have to decide where I want to divide this nerve to excise it.
So there's the schwannoma.
Put it in a anchor bag and remove it through the assistant port.
And the last thing I did was I clipped the end of this nerve
Some of that is if we needed to give adjuvant radiation at the end.
At least we know where the nerve was divided.
The patient actually did quite well.
She had what I would consider a mild Horner syndrome after surgery and now it's almost a year after surgery and her ptosis has resolved.
She still has
a little bit of meiosis and she has anhydrous of her right hand.
But she's doing quite well and she's had no recurrence.
